perturbation.original_text: This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement (including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of actions), shall be governed by and cons trued in accordance with the laws of the State Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement Page 4 of Oregon, notwithstanding any conflict-of -laws doctrines of such state or other jurisdiction to the contrary, and without th e aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement and all questions relating to its validity, interpretation, performance and enforcement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Oregon, notwithstanding any conflict-of-laws doctrines of such state or other jurisdiction to the contrary, and without the aid of any canon, custom or rule of law requiring construction against the draftsman.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'including, without limitation, provisions concerning limitations of actions,' the agreement becomes ambiguous regarding the statute of limitations applicable to disputes. Oregon law has specific statutes of limitations for various claims, and this omission creates uncertainty, potentially harming the Receiving Party if the Disclosing Party delays bringing a claim beyond the legally allowed period. The clause is weakened, which opens up the possibility of the law being contradicted.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oregon Revised Statute (ORS) § 12.080 - Contract Statute of Limitations
perturbation.law_explanation: ORS § 12.080 establishes a six-year statute of limitations for actions upon contracts. By omitting the specific reference to 'provisions concerning limitations of actions' from the governing law clause, the contract introduces ambiguity about whether this statute of limitations applies, creating a potential contradiction and legal risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ORS 12.080 – Action on certain contracts or liabilities ORS 12.080 Action on certain contracts or liabilities Text Annotations (1) An action upon a contract or liability, express or implied, excepting those mentioned in ORS 12.070 (Action on judgment, decree or sealed instrument) , 12.110 (Actions for certain injuries to person not arising on contract) and 12.135 (Action for damages from construction, alteration or repair of improvement to real property) and except as otherwise provided in ORS 72.7250 (Statute of limitations in contracts for sale) ; (2) An action upon a liability created by statute, other than a penalty or forfeiture, excepting those mentioned in ORS 12.110 (Actions for certain injuries to person not arising on contract) ; (3) An action for waste or trespass upon or for interference with or injury to any interest of another in real property, excepting those mentioned in ORS 12.050 (Action to recover real property) , 12.060 (Suit or action on land contracts) , 12.135 (Action for damages from construction, alteration or repair of improvement to real property) , 12.137 (Action for loss of or damage to property arising from nuclear incident) and 273.241 (Action to recover damages for unlawful removal of material) ; or (4) An action for taking, detaining or injuring personal property, including an action for the specific recovery thereof, excepting an action mentioned in ORS 12.137 (Action for loss of or damage to property arising from nuclear incident) ; shall be commenced within six years. [Amended by 1957 c.374 §3; 1961 c.726 §396; 1973 c.363 §1; 1983 c.437 §2; 1987 c.705 §3; 1991 c.968 §2] Source: Section 12.080 — Action on certain contracts or liabilities , https://www.­oregonlegislature.­gov/bills_laws/ors/ors012.­html (accessed May 26, 2025). 12.010 Time of commencing actions 12.020 When action deemed begun 12.040 Limitations of suits generally 12.050 Action to recover real property 12.060 Suit or action on land contracts 12.070 Action on judgment, decree o
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly includes 'provisions concerning limitations of actions', ensuring clarity regarding the statute of limitations. The changed text removes this phrase, creating ambiguity about whether ORS § 12.080, which establishes a six-year statute of limitations for contract actions, applies. This omission introduces uncertainty, potentially harming the Receiving Party if the Disclosing Party delays bringing a claim beyond the legally allowed period. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (ORS 12.080) confirms that actions upon a contract shall be commenced within six years, highlighting the legal requirement for a statute of limitations. By omitting the reference to limitations of actions, the contract contradicts the intention of ORS 12.080, creating a legal loophole and uncertainty.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party. When the Receiving Party has finished reviewing the information provided by the Disclosing Party and has made a decision as to whet her or not to work with the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all information to the Disclosing Party without retaining any copies.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party agrees to return all Confidential Information to Disclosing Party within 15 days of written demand by Disclosing Party. When the Receiving Party has finished reviewing the information provided by the Disclosing Party, Receiving Party shall return all information to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'and has made a decision as to whet her or not to work with the Disclosing Party... without retaining any copies,' the agreement becomes ambiguous regarding when the receiving party is supposed to return the documents. This also opens up the possibility that the receiving party makes a decision to not work with the disclosing party, and retains all copies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oregon Uniform Trade Secrets Act (OUTSA) - ORS 646.461 to 646.475
perturbation.law_explanation: ORS 646.463 addresses misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes unauthorized disclosure or use. While the contract intends to protect confidential information, the edited clause removes a clear obligation to return all copies of the information. This omission weakens the protection against misappropriation, potentially conflicting with the intent of the OUTSA by failing to explicitly prevent the Receiving Party from retaining copies of the trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: ORS 646.463 – Enjoining misappropriation; payment of royalties; affirmative acts ORS 646.463 Enjoining misappropriation payment of royalties affirmative acts Text Annotations (1) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be temporarily, preliminarily or permanently enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be vacated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of the misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. (3) In appropriate circumstances, the court may order affirmative acts to protect a trade secret. [1987 c.537 §3] Source: Section 646.463 — Enjoining misappropriation; payment of royalties; affirmative acts , https://www.­oregonlegislature.­gov/bills_laws/ors/ors646.­html (accessed May 26, 2025). 646.010 Designation and scope of ORS 646.010 to 646.180 646.020 Definitions and explanations 646.030 Application to cooperative associations 646.040 Price discrimination prohibited 646.050 Establishing prima facie case of discrimination 646.060 Commissions and allowances 646.070 Special payments to customers 646.080 Special services to customers 646.090 Inducing or receiving price discrimination prohibited 646.130 Cost surveys as evidence 646.140 Enjoining violations 646.150 Action for damages 646.160 Presumption of damages 646.170 Requiring defendant to testify 646.180 Illegal contracts 646.461 Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475 646.463 Enjoining misappropriation 646.465 Damages for misappropriation 646.467 Attorney fees 646.469 Pres

Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be vacated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional reasonable period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (2) In exceptional circumstances, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for the period of time for which use could have been prohibited. Exceptional circumstances include, but are not limited to, a material and prejudicial change of position prior to acquiring knowledge or reason to know of the misappropriation that renders a prohibitive injunction inequitable. [1987 c.537 §3] Source: Section 646.463 — Enjoining misappropriation; payment of royalties; affirmative acts , https://www.­oregonlegislature.­gov/bills_laws/ors/ors646.­html (accessed May 26, 2025). 646.010 Designation and scope of ORS 646.010 to 646.180 646.020 Definitions and explanations 646.030 Application to cooperative associations 646.040 Price discrimination prohibited 646.050 Establishing prima facie case of discrimination 646.060 Commissions and allowances 646.070 Special payments to customers 646.080 Special services to customers 646.090 Inducing or receiving price discrimination prohibited 646.130 Cost surveys as evidence 646.140 Enjoining violations 646.150 Action for damages 646.160 Presumption of damages 646.170 Requiring defendant to testify 646.180 Illegal contracts 646.461 Definitions for ORS 646.461 to 646.475 646.463 Enjoining misappropriation 646.465 Damages for misappropriation 646.467 Attorney fees 646.469 Pres
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the clause requiring the Receiving Party to return all copies of confidential information after making a decision whether to work with the Disclosing Party. This omission weakens the protection against misappropriation, potentially conflicting with the intent of the OUTSA. ORS 646.463 allows for injunctions against misappropriation, implying a legal expectation to prevent unauthorized use or disclosure. By removing the obligation to return all copies, the edited contract creates a loophole where the Receiving Party could retain copies of trade secrets, thus potentially leading to misappropriation. The scraped snippets support that ORS 646.463 allows for injunctions to prevent misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the forego ing, either party may seek and obtain temporary injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may seek injunctive relief from a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the word 'temporary' and 'against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information,' the agreement loses the limited scope of the type of remedy and the reason it would be given. This may mean that in practice, courts cannot act as swiftly to stop improper use of confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Oregon Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 65
perturbation.law_explanation: ORCP 65 outlines the requirements for obtaining a preliminary injunction, including demonstrating irreparable harm and a likelihood of success on the merits. By removing the phrase 'against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information', the agreement does not explicitly outline a legal scope. This contradiction would make it harder to prove those items, or at least makes it more ambiguous.

perturbation.justification: The original contract limited injunctive relief to 'temporary' situations concerning 'improper disclosure of Confidential Information.' The modified text broadens this to any injunctive relief. The Law Explanation states that by removing 'against any improper disclosure of the Confidential Information,' the agreement doesn't explicitly outline a legal scope, contradicting ORCP 65's requirements for preliminary injunctions, which include demonstrating irreparable harm and likelihood of success. Without the limitations, proving these elements becomes more ambiguous and difficult. The scraped snippet does not work.

perturbation.original_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration conducted by the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Asso ciation and in accordance with the rules thereof, or in any other convenient forum agreed to in writing by the parties. Any arbitration award shall be final and binding, and judgment upon the award rendered pursuant to such arbitration may be entered in any court of proper jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Any controversy or claim arising out of or relating to this agreement, or the breach thereof, shall be resolved by arbitration conducted in any convenient forum agreed to in writing by the parties. Any arbitration award shall be final and binding.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause 'Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Association and in accordance with the rules thereof' and 'judgment upon the award rendered pursuant to such arbitration may be entered in any court of proper jurisdiction,' the agreement fails to adequately set up how the process should occur. The American Arbitration Association (AAA) has specific consumer arbitration rules in place, and the original text provided a strong enforcement mechanism for judgements. Removing the link to the AAA and the judgment enforcement clause weakens consumer protection. Now, there is less transparency, and there is less of a guarantee that the court will enforce the law.
perturbation.law_explanation: ORS 36.705 dictates procedures for enforcing arbitration agreements. By removing the language that arbitration will be performed by the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Association and in accordance with the rules thereof, the modified text opens up the process to other arbitration standards. ORS 36.705 protects the rights of people in this kind of arbitration agreement. Leaving out the rules might make the agreement harder to enforce.

service as neutral arbitrator 36.650 Disclosure by arbitrator 36.655 Action by majority 36.660 Immunity of arbitrator; competency to testify; attorney fees and costs 36.665 Arbitration process 36.670 Representation by a lawyer; representation of legal or commercial entities 36.675 Witnesses; subpoenas; depositions; discovery 36.680 Judicial enforcement of preaward ruling by arbitrator 36.685 Award 36.690 Change of award by arbitrator 36.695 Remedies; fees and expenses of arbitration proceeding 36.700 Confirmation of award 36.705 Vacating award 36.710 Modification or correction of award 36.715 Judgment on award; attorney fees and litigation expenses 36.720 Jurisdiction 36.725 Venue 36.730 Appeals 36.735 Uniformity of application and construction 36.740 Relationship to electronic signatures in Global and National Commerce Act DISPUTE RESOLUTION (Generally) 36.100 Policy for ORS 36.100 to 36.238. It is the policy and purpose of ORS 36.100 to 36.238 that, when two or more persons cannot settle a dispute directly between themselves, it is preferable that the disputants be encouraged and assisted to resolve their dispute with the assistance of a trusted and competent third party mediator, whenever possible, rather than the dispute remaining unresolved or resulting in litigation. [1989 c.718 §1; 2003 c.791 §9] 36.105 Declaration of purpose of ORS 36.100 to 36.238. The Legislative Assembly declares that it is the purpose of ORS 36.100 to 36.238 to: (1) Foster the development of community-based programs that will assist citizens in resolving disputes and developing skills in conflict resolution; (2) Allow flexible and diverse programs to be developed in this state, to meet specific needs in local areas and to benefit this state as a whole through experiments using a variety of models of peaceful dispute resolution; (3) Find alternative methods for addressing the needs of crime victims in criminal cases when those cases are either not prosecuted for lack of funds or can be more efficiently handled outside the courts; (4) Provide a method to evaluate the effect of dispute resolution programs on communities, local governments, the justice system and state agencies; (5) Encourage the development and use of mediation panels for resolution of civil litigation disputes; (6) Foster the development or expansion of integrated, flexible and diverse state agency programs that involve state and local agencies and the public and that provide for use of alternative means of dispute resolution pursuant to ORS 183.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated arbitration through the Commercial Division of the American Arbitration Association (AAA), ensuring adherence to its rules and providing a clear path for judgment enforcement. The changed text eliminates this specific requirement, opening the door to potentially less regulated or less enforceable arbitration processes. This omission contradicts the intent of ORS 36.705, which, as suggested by the Law Explanation, aims to protect the rights of parties involved in arbitration agreements. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't explicitly state this protection, it lists ORS 36.705 under the 'Uniform Arbitration Act,' suggesting that this act and its related statutes, including ORS 36.705, are designed to set standards for fair arbitration. Removing the reference to AAA rules weakens this consumer protection by creating ambiguity about which rules govern the process, making the agreement harder to enforce and thus contradicting the purpose of ORS 36.705. The omission is not a minor weakening but a significant removal of a specific, established arbitration framework.
